message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The present study explored public’s willingness to use COVID-19 immunity certificates across six different domestic scenarios.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cross-sectional online survey.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    UK representative survey conducted on the 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    of August 2021.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>534 UK residents over 18 years old.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interventions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants replied to the same set of questions.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Primary and secondary outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The primary outcome measure was
                    <jats:italic>willingness to use</jats:italic>
                    immunity certificates across three different domestic settings (1. Visiting the GP for a non-urgent health issue, 2. Dining in a restaurant, and 3. Attending a performance in a theatre). For each setting two options, one prioritising
                    <jats:italic>convenience</jats:italic>
                    (option A) and the other
                    <jats:italic>privacy</jats:italic>
                    (option B), were offered. Our secondary outcome measures were computed indices from items adapted from the Health Belief Model; Attitudes towards sharing immunity status with service providers; Prior to COVID-19 lifestyle. In addition, we recorded data about respondents’ socio-demographic characteristics.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Respondents were more willing to use immunity certificates that prioritised
                    <jats:italic>convenience</jats:italic>
                    , rather than
                    <jats:italic>privacy</jats:italic>
                    , when visiting their GP (92%). However, privacy was more favorable (84%) in the other two settings (dining in a restaurant and going to a theatre) compared to
                    <jats:italic>convenience</jats:italic>
                    (39%). Personal beliefs about COVID-19 and immunity certificates were associated with variations in willingness to use these across all scenarios. No variations were observed across socio-demographics and lifestyle.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The findings of this survey suggest that there is not
                    <jats:italic>one size fits all</jats:italic>
                    solution for designing immunity certificates. Immunity certificates are complex socio-technical systems, any attempt to implement these for domestic use should be tailored to different settings and user needs. The design of certification services requires a more evidence-based approach and further research is needed to understand how different settings, design elements (like
                    <jats:italic>convenience</jats:italic>
                    or
                    <jats:italic>privacy</jats:italic>
                    ) and personal beliefs about the pandemic should inform their design.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This study presents unique knowledge about the public’s willingness to use immunity certificates for domestic purposes where there is very little published research at the moment.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The study reports knowledge about the interaction between individual characteristics, domestic settings, and types of immunity certificate design on willingness to use these certificates.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>UK nationally representative sample for age, gender, and ethnic background, but limited to people who have the means and capacity to use digital technologies (survey administered using Prolific.co).</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We present evidence-based recommendations for Public Health services and policy makers about the use of immunity certificates in different domestic settings.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Since, as to the writing of this paper, COVID-19 certification has not been mandated in the UK, the scenarios used in the survey were hypothetical.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Why “one size fits all” is not enough when designing COVID-19 immunity certificates for domestic use: a UK wide cross-sectional online survey

reference.unstructured: Ada Lovelace Institute. The data divide: Public attitudes to tackling social and health inequalitites in the COVID-19 pandemic and beyond. 2021.
